scenario.context: Station 02: business requirements and as-is analysis for different site sizes.
scenario.mission: You structure requirements, distinguish network sizes, and derive a solid starting picture from them.

constraints: Connect company size with technical consequences
Keep need and as-is picture clearly separate
Show planning reserves in a traceable way

customerRequest.subject: Station 02: Requirements and as-is analysis
customerRequest.body: Please prepare a solid requirements and as-is view for three site types.

questions.title: Prioritize business requirements
questions.prompt: Which information affects network planning the most?

options.text: Whether an existing switch still looks good
options.rationale: Has almost no impact on the target architecture.

options.text: Which desk layout is preferred in the office
options.rationale: Only indirectly relevant to network design.

options.text: Which services are critical, how many users need to be served, and what growth is expected
options.rationale: That is exactly what determines capacity, segmentation, and redundancy needs.

feedback.correct: Correct: Services, user count, and growth drive network planning.
feedback.wrong: Not suitable: Technically relevant business requirements must be clear first.

questions.title: Distinguish site sizes
questions.prompt: Which criteria help distinguish small, medium, and large networks in a meaningful way? (Multiple choice)

options.text: Number of locations and users to be served
options.rationale: This is a central scaling factor.

options.text: Criticality of services and required availability
options.rationale: This strongly shapes the technical design.

options.text: Whether the company logo looks modern
options.rationale: Has no technical relevance for network size.
Directly affects design and operational effort.
Is not a reliable planning criterion.

feedback.correct: Correct: Network size is determined by operational needs, not by surface details.
feedback.wrong: Partially wrong: Please weigh users, services, and protection needs more strongly.

questions.title: Build as-is analysis systematically
questions.prompt: Put the steps of a clean as-is analysis in order.
items: Capture existing components and cabling paths
Condense the result for the requirements specification
correctOrder: Capture existing components and cabling paths
Condense the result for the requirements specification

feedback.correct: Correct: The as-is analysis leads logically to target-state preparation.
feedback.wrong: Not ideal: Please move from the existing state through risks to a condensed result.

questions.title: Branch site port planning
questions.prompt: A branch site needs 24 notebooks, 8 VoIP phones, 4 printers, and 2 access points. How many ports should be planned with a 20% reserve? Round up to whole ports.

feedback.wrong: Please account for all devices and the reserve correctly.

questions.title: From as-is to to-be
questions.prompt: Describe in 4-6 sentences how you would derive a prioritized target picture for the requirements specification from the as-is analysis.
questions.expectedOutline: Derivation of a prioritized target picture from the existing state, deviation, and risk.

feedback.correct: Correct: The path from the as-is view to the target picture is explained well.
feedback.wrong: Please connect the as-is picture, target assumption, and prioritization more clearly.
